justification: All six pass criteria met: (1) Distributed tracing gap explicitly identified as cross-service diagnosis blocker — called '#1 gap to close' and detailed in tracing section, component_interactions, and escalations. (2) 30-second log delay identified as incident response concern — noted as 'introduces a blind spot during fast-moving incidents'. (3) Stale 18-month runbook flagged as liability — 'stale runbooks are worse than no runbook because they give false confidence'. (4) No recovery tooling for stuck orders identified — extensively covered across resilience_assessment and escalations, noting manual DB intervention is the only path. (5) Multiple specific recommendations provided: distributed tracing, runbook quarterly review cadence, admin API/CLI for safe manual state transitions, dead-letter queue, outbox pattern, burn-rate alerting. (6) Verdict is 'NOT READY'.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough incident response assessment — goes well beyond monitoring coverage to assess what an on-call engineer can actually do at 3am
Correctly frames distributed tracing as a diagnosis capability gap, not just a missing feature
Stale runbook analysis is nuanced — identifies it as actively harmful rather than merely outdated
Recovery tooling gap is covered from multiple angles: no replay, no dead-letter, no admin endpoint, no bulk recovery
Goes beyond rubric requirements by identifying additional gaps: no circuit breakers, no stuck-order gauge metric, no retry logic, no SLO-based alerting
The debugging_capability narrative effectively synthesizes gaps into a concrete on-call scenario
Correctly notes alert threshold and PagerDuty routing are adequate while identifying their limitations
